claim: [Whether productivity shocks from technological advances follow historical patterns of job creation] AI will cause net job losses in the next decade, particularly in white-collar work, with displaced workers unable to transition to new roles and experiencing wage decline. vs. AI advancement/proliferation causes/supports unemployment rate increase vs. AI capabilities in white-collar work causes/supports job displacement in US economy vs. AI being general intelligence improving at human tasks causes/supports traditional job redeployment strategy failing vs. AI will not cause net job losses in the next decade because historical technological revolutions create productivity gains that generate new industries and labor demand, despite near-term displacement and distributional challenges. vs. technological revolutions undermines/reduces labor elimination vs. Microsoft Office advent causes/supports office worker labor demand vs. major technological advances causes/supports productivity shocks pattern vs. technological revolutions causes/supports task composition alteration
settlingQuestion: What will be the net change in total employment (employed persons) in the United States between 2024 and 2034, measured as a percentage change in the Bureau of Labor Statistics' total nonfarm employment figure?

settlingQuestion: What percentage of workers displaced by AI automation over the next 10 years find new employment in roles with median wages at or above their previous position, broken down by education level and occupational category?

settlingQuestion: What percentage of workers displaced by AI automation between 2020-2025 successfully transitioned to AI management, oversight, or complementary roles versus those who experienced sustained unemployment or underemployment?
